concepts.title: Explain the core concepts of Unix File IO in interesting ways! Use illustrative diagrams where required.
You can refer to the base material here, but feel free to do your research.

images.prompt: A striking, modern editorial illustration in flat vector style: a vintage Unix terminal (amber-on-black CRT) on the left, from which a single glowing teal ribbon of bytes — little 1s and 0s riding the ribbon — flows rightward through a short brass pipe, then forks to feed three different destinations at once: a filing cabinet (a file), a keyboard (a terminal), and a network socket jack. The visual punchline is that it is the SAME ribbon feeding all three. Cool manual-page paper background with a faint blueprint grid, palette of pipe-teal + brass-amber + slate-blue with a terminal-green accent, generous whitespace, soft depth, no text baked in. Mood: one simple idea quietly powering everything. Target size ~1280x720px, 16:9 landscape; compose with the subject centred and safe margins — it is letterboxed into a 16:9 box, so keep nothing important near the edges.
images.alt: An old Unix terminal from which a glowing stream of bytes flows through a pipe into a filing cabinet, a keyboard, and a network jack — all the same stream
images.caption: One stream of bytes, many destinations — the whole book in one picture.

summary: Everything is a file: how open, read, write, close, lseek, dup2, and pipes turn one tiny idea into files, terminals, and shell pipelines alike.
title: Unix File IO
